name: Ar-Rafi

names.en: The Exalter, the One who raises
The Abaser, the One who brings low

title: Ar-Rafi: The Exalter, the One who raises
meta_description: Ar-Rafi, the One who raises, built from the Qur'an and classical tafsir. The Hour that lowers the proud and lifts the humble, the God who raises the believers and the people of knowledge by degrees, and the mention He raised so high it is said beside His own. Qur'an verified.
keywords: Ar-Rafi, Ar-Raafi, The Exalter, Al-Khafid, names of Allah, 99 names, Allah raises the humble, Surah Al-Waqia 56:3, rafi al-darajat, We raise in degrees whom We will, Asma ul Husna, Buruja
source_note: Grounded in the Qur'an (Sahih International, verified via quran.ai) and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir and Tafsir as-Sa'di), in the voice of Buruja. The definite name-form الرَّافِع does not appear in the Qur'an; it rests on the 99-names tradition and is grounded here through the verb and the construct as they occur in the Book.
lede: Almost everyone carries a private ledger of where they rank. We feel it in the room we are afraid to walk into, the name that gets called before ours, the quiet sense that other people have been lifted and we have been left on the floor. We spend a great deal of our lives trying to climb, and an exhausting amount of energy trying not to be brought low. This name walks straight into that anxiety and tells you who actually holds the rope.

Ar-Rafi, the One who raises. The Qur'an does not leave rank in human hands. It teaches that there is One who lowers and One who lifts, and that they are the same One, so that no throne is secure without Him and no floor is final with Him. The scholars place this name beside its companion, Al-Khafid, the One who brings low, because you cannot understand the lifting without the lowering. And the most startling thing the tafsir says about both is who gets raised: not the loud and the high, but, again and again, the humble.

sections.h2: The name, and the name that answers it

ayah.text: It will bring down [some] and raise up [others].

paras: Begin with one honest thing about this name. The definite form you will see in the list of ninety-nine, Ar-Rafi, the One who raises, does not appear in the Qur'an as a standing name of Allah the way As-Sami or Al-Alim do. What the Qur'an gives is the action and the description: the verb rafa'a, to raise, used of Allah over and over, and the construct rafi' al-darajat, the One who raises ranks. The name itself comes to us through the tradition of the ninety-nine, built faithfully on what the Book says Allah does. So when we say Ya Rafi, we are naming Allah by a deed He claims for Himself on nearly every page, even though He did not coin this exact title for Himself in a verse. We want you to know the seam, not paper over it.

Ar-Rafi comes from three Arabic letters, ra, fa, ayn, the root of raf', raising and elevation. And it almost never stands alone. The scholars set it beside Al-Khafid, from kha, fa, dad, the One who lowers, and the Qur'an itself binds the two in a single breath. In Surah Al-Waqia, describing the Day of Judgement, the whole event is summed up in two words: khafidatun rafi'ah, lowering, raising. One Hand does both.

Sit with how much that single verse undoes. We imagine status is something we negotiate with each other, a thing you accumulate and defend. The Qur'an says the final word on every rank belongs to the One who can lower and lift at will. That is terrifying if you have built your whole worth on being above other people. It is the best news in the world if you have spent your life underneath them.

sections.h2: What it means that Allah raises

ayah.arabic: رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجَاتِ ذُو الْعَرْشِ يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ لِيُنذِرَ يَوْمَ التَّلَاقِ
ayah.text: [He is] the Exalted above [all] degrees, Owner of the Throne; He places the inspiration of His command [i.e., revelation] upon whom He wills of His servants to warn of the Day of Meeting.

paras: The raising in this name moves in two directions at once, and you have to hold both. First, Allah is in Himself the most high, raised above His creation in a way nothing shares. Commenting on this verse, al-Sa'di explains rafi' al-darajat as the Exalted, the Most High, whose degrees are raised so far above His creatures that His attributes are majestic and His very being is exalted, so that none may draw near to Him except through pure and sincere works. Ibn Kathir, reading the same words, speaks of Allah's greatness and the loftiness of His mighty Throne, raised over all His creation like a roof above it. Before this name ever lifts you, it tells you who is genuinely at the top, and it is not anyone you were comparing yourself to.

Second, and this is the tender turn, the same verse that names Him the Exalted above all degrees immediately shows Him reaching down: He places the inspiration of His command upon whom He wills of His servants. Al-Sa'di draws the line out explicitly, that the works which raise a servant's degrees and bring him near and set him above the rest are sincerity and pure devotion. So the God who is highest is the God who lifts, and the ladder He lowers is not wealth or fame but a clean heart. The One on the Throne stoops to raise the sincere toward Himself.

We might pause here and notice, simply as a reflection on these two glimpses, how unlike us this is. When we are high, we guard the distance. The Qur'an shows Allah at the absolute height and, in the same breath, sending down what raises His servants. Highness in Him is not withdrawal. It is the very position from which He lifts.

sections.h2: He lowers the proud and raises the humble

ayah.text: It will bring down [some] and raise up [others].

paras: Now go back to that two-word verse and let the tafsir tell you who ends up where, because it overturns the order we are used to. Ibn Kathir gathers the early explanations of khafidatun rafi'ah and they point one way. He relates from al-Hasan and Qatada that the Hour lowers some peoples down to the lowest depths, into the Fire, even if they were mighty in this world, and raises others to the highest heights, into lasting bliss, even if they were lowly in this world. He carries Muhammad ibn Ka'b's words: it brings down men who were elevated in the dunya, and raises up men who were brought low in the dunya. And he preserves al-Suddi's gloss in its sharpest form: it lowers the arrogant, and it raises the humble.

Stop on that last sentence. Khafadat al-mutakabbirin wa rafa'at al-mutawadi'in, it lowers the arrogant and raises the humble. That is the logic of Ar-Rafi laid bare. The currency that buys height with Allah is the exact opposite of the currency the world runs on. The world lifts the one who pushes to the front. Allah, on the Day everything is made true, lifts the one who was content to be at the back for His sake. Ibn Kathir relays from Uthman ibn Saraqa that the Hour lowered the enemies of Allah into the Fire and raised the friends of Allah into the Garden. Where you stand on that Day is set by something invisible now: not your position, but your posture.

There is a second, quieter reading the mufassirun mention, and it is worth knowing because it deepens the first. Al-Sa'di notes the verse can also describe the Voice of that Day, lowering so the near can hear it and rising so the far can hear it. Ibn Kathir carries the same from Ibn Abbas and Ikrima: it brought the near to hear and the far to hear. Whether the verse is lowering people and raising people, or lowering its sound and raising its sound, the nerve is the same. On that Day nothing escapes the leveling and the lifting of Ar-Rafi. The whole arrangement of who-is-above-whom is taken out of our hands and put back in His.

sections.h2: The ladder He raises you by

ayah.arabic: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمْ تَفَسَّحُوا فِي الْمَجَالِسِ فَافْسَحُوا يَفْسَحِ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۖ وَإِذَا قِيلَ انشُزُوا فَانشُزُوا يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجَاتٍ ۚ وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ
ayah.text: O you who have believed, when you are told, "Space yourselves" in assemblies, then make space; Allah will make space for you. And when you are told, "Arise," then arise; Allah will raise those who have believed among you and those who were given knowledge, by degrees. And Allah is Aware of what you do.

ayah.arabic: نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَاتٍ مَّن نَّشَاءُ ۗ وَفَوْقَ كُلِّ ذِي عِلْمٍ عَلِيمٌ
ayah.text: We raise in degrees whom We will, but over every possessor of knowledge is one [more] knowing.

paras: If Allah raises the humble, what does the raising actually look like, and what is the rope made of? The Qur'an answers in Surah Al-Mujadila, and the setting is almost comically ordinary: a crowded gathering, people asked to budge over and make room. Allah will raise those who have believed among you and those who were given knowledge, by degrees. The ladder is named outright here. It is faith and knowledge.

What moves me is the verse before the lifting. Ibn Kathir explains the scene: the believers are told to make space for one another, and not to think that yielding their seat is a loss. Do not imagine, he writes, that making room for your brother or rising when you are asked to rise is any diminishment of your worth. Rather it is elevation and rank with Allah, and Allah does not let it go to waste. Then he states the principle of the whole name in one line: whoever humbles himself for the command of Allah, Allah raises his rank and spreads his mention. There it is again. You go lower, on purpose, for His sake, and that is the exact motion by which He takes you higher. The seat you gave up was never the height. Giving it up was.

And the height of knowledge is real. Al-Sa'di, commenting on We raise in degrees whom We will in the story of Yusuf, says Allah raises the possessor of beneficial knowledge above the servants by degrees, and makes the one who knows and acts and teaches an imam for the people, whose footsteps are followed and by whose light others walk in the dark. Ibn Kathir, on the same theme, carries Umar ibn al-Khattab's report of the Prophet's words: indeed Allah raises peoples by this Book, and lowers others by it. The Qur'an is itself a thing that lifts and lowers, depending on what you do with it.

But the Qur'an guards this gift from becoming a new kind of pride, and it does so beautifully. Right after We raise in degrees whom We will, it adds: but over every possessor of knowledge is one more knowing. Al-Sa'di explains that above every scholar is someone with more knowledge, on and on, until knowledge ends in the One who knows the unseen and the seen. So the very thing that raises you also keeps you humble, because however high Ar-Rafi lifts you, there is always Someone higher, and at the top of every ladder is Allah.

sections.h2: The mention He raised so high

ayah.text: And raised high for you your repute.

paras: To see what Ar-Rafi can do, look at the highest thing He ever raised. To His Prophet ﷺ, who began as an orphan in Makkah, mocked and driven out, Allah said: and We raised high for you your mention. Ibn Kathir relates the meaning Mujahid gave it, that Allah said, I am not mentioned except that you are mentioned with Me: there is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah. Qatada adds that Allah raised his mention in this world and the next, so there is no speaker, no one bearing witness, no one at prayer, but proclaims it. Al-Sa'di sums it: Allah raised his worth and gave him praise so high that no one in creation has reached it, so that Allah is not remembered without His Messenger being remembered alongside Him.

Hold the shape of that life against the name. The man whose mention was lifted to the call of every prayer until the end of time was the same man who sat where the gathering ended, who answered cruelty with patience, who never once raised himself. Ar-Rafi took the lowest beginning the world could offer and turned it into the highest mention a human has ever held. The pattern is unmistakable, and it is the pattern of this whole name: He raises from below.

He did it for others too. Ibn Kathir's commentary on the man who knew the Book is one example of it in life: Umar appointed a freed slave, Ibn Abza, over the people of Makkah, and when challenged for raising a mawla over the nobles, he answered that the man was a reciter of the Book of Allah and learned in its rulings, and then quoted the Prophet ﷺ, that Allah raises peoples by this Book. And al-Sa'di notes that Allah raised the Prophet Idris too, where the Qur'an says, and We raised him to a high station, lifting his mention among the worlds and his rank among those brought near. The All-Raising has a long record. He keeps lifting the ones the world overlooked.

sections.h2: Live as someone Ar-Rafi can raise
paras: A name of Allah is never only something to know. It is meant to change how you stand, and Ar-Rafi changes you in at least three ways.

First, it pulls the poison of comparison out of your chest. If rank is in the Hand of the One who lowers and lifts, then the ladder you have been frantically climbing against other people was never the real one. You do not have to claw your way above anyone, and you do not have to dread being below them, because the only height that lasts is the one Ar-Rafi grants, and He grants it, as al-Suddi read the verse, to the humble. The pressure to be seen as high can come off. You answer to the One who sets the true ranks.

Second, it tells you exactly how to rise, and the instructions are upside down. Ibn Kathir's line on Surah Al-Mujadila is the whole method: whoever humbles himself for the command of Allah, Allah raises his rank. So lower yourself on purpose. Make room. Take the lesser seat. Serve the person no one is serving. Seek the knowledge and the faith that the Qur'an names as the ladder. Each of those is a step down in the eyes of the room and a step up in the sight of Allah. You climb by stooping.

Third, it changes how you treat the people the world has placed underneath you. The God you worship is the One who lifts the lowly and lowers the proud, who raised a freed slave over the nobles of Makkah for his knowledge, who made an orphan's name the most spoken on earth. A heart shaped by Ar-Rafi cannot keep looking down on anyone, because it has seen who Allah chooses to raise. You start treating the overlooked the way the All-Raising treats them, knowing that the one beneath you today may be far above you on the Day that the lowering and the lifting are made true.

sections.h2: The Hand that lifts the lowly
paras: Step back and let the mercy in this name settle. There is a fear underneath a lot of our striving, that we are stuck where we are, unseen and unlifted, while everyone else is carried up. Ar-Rafi answers it, not with a slogan but with the way He actually runs His creation. The God on the Throne, raised above every degree, is the very One who reaches down to lift. And the ones He lifts are not the ones standing tallest. As the mufassirun read Surah Al-Waqia, on the Day that makes everything true, He lowers the arrogant and raises the humble.

So you are not trapped at the bottom of anything. The seat you were given up, the name that went uncalled, the times you chose to go low for the sake of Allah and no one noticed, none of it was lost. Ibn Kathir said it plainly: whoever humbles himself for Allah, Allah raises him and spreads his mention. The motion of this whole name is upward, and it begins from below. Ar-Rafi does not lift those who lifted themselves. He lifts the ones who lowered themselves to Him.

O Allah, Ar-Rafi, the One who raises, You lower whom You will and lift whom You will, and the final word on every rank is Yours. Lift us by faith and by knowledge, raise us in the only degrees that last, and let us be among the humble that You raise and not the proud that You bring low. Grant us authority, and join us with the righteous, and place us among the inheritors of the Garden of bliss.

dua.arabic: رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ ۝ وَاجْعَلْنِي مِن وَرَثَةِ جَنَّةِ النَّعِيمِ
dua.translit: Rabbi hab li hukman wa alhiqni bis-salihin, waj'alni min warathati jannatin-na'im
dua.en: My Lord, grant me authority and join me with the righteous. And place me among the inheritors of the Garden of Pleasure.

lessons.bold: Hand your rank back to the One who sets it.
lessons.text: The Qur'an puts the final word on every status in Allah's hand: lowering, raising, in one breath (56:3). You do not have to climb above anyone or dread being below them, because the only height that lasts is the one Ar-Rafi grants.

Ibn Kathir's rule on Surah Al-Mujadila is the whole method: whoever humbles himself for the command of Allah, Allah raises his rank and spreads his mention. Take the lesser seat, make room, serve quietly. Each step down in the room is a step up with Allah.

lessons.bold: Take the ladder the Qur'an actually names.
lessons.text: Allah raises the believers and those given knowledge by degrees (58:11). Al-Sa'di says beneficial knowledge lifts its people above the rest and makes them imams others follow. Seek the faith and the knowledge that He lifts by.

lessons.bold: Let knowledge keep you humble, not proud.
lessons.text: Right after 'We raise in degrees whom We will,' the verse adds, 'over every possessor of knowledge is one more knowing' (12:76). However high you are lifted, Someone is always higher, until knowledge ends in Allah.

lessons.bold: Honour the ones the world looks past.
lessons.text: The God you worship raised a freed slave over the nobles of Makkah for his knowledge, and made an orphan's mention the most spoken on earth (94:4). A heart shaped by Ar-Rafi cannot look down on anyone He may be about to raise.

faq.q: What does the name Ar-Rafi mean?
faq.a: Ar-Rafi (الرافع) means The Exalter, the One who raises, from the root r-f-' (raising, elevation). It carries two senses the mufassirun draw out: that Allah is in Himself the Most High, exalted above all degrees (rafi' al-darajat, 40:15, as al-Sa'di and Ibn Kathir explain), and that He raises His servants in rank, lifting the believers and the people of knowledge by degrees (58:11). Reading Surah Al-Waqia 56:3, the scholars note He raises the humble and lowers the arrogant.

faq.q: Is Ar-Rafi actually in the Qur'an?
faq.a: The definite name-form الرافع does not appear in the Qur'an as a standing name of Allah. What the Qur'an gives is the action and the description: the verb rafa'a, 'to raise,' used of Allah repeatedly (for example 58:11, 6:83, 94:4, 19:57), and the construct rafi' al-darajat, 'the One who raises ranks' (40:15). The name Ar-Rafi comes through the tradition of the ninety-nine names, built faithfully on what the Book repeatedly says Allah does, rather than on a single verse that titles Him by this exact word. We have grounded it here through the verb and the construct as they occur.

faq.q: Why is Ar-Rafi paired with Al-Khafid?
faq.a: Because the Qur'an itself binds them. Surah Al-Waqia describes the Day of Judgement in two words, khafidatun rafi'ah, 'lowering, raising' (56:3), one event done by one Hand. Al-Khafid, the One who brings low, is the companion name (#22 in the traditional list), and you cannot grasp the lifting without the lowering. Ibn Kathir gathers the early readings of this verse: the Hour lowers the mighty of this world and raises the lowly, and as al-Suddi put it, 'it lowers the arrogant and raises the humble.'

faq.q: How does Allah raise a person, according to the tafsir?
faq.a: By faith and by knowledge, and through humility. Commenting on 'Allah will raise those who have believed among you and those who were given knowledge, by degrees' (58:11), Ibn Kathir states the principle: whoever humbles himself for the command of Allah, Allah raises his rank and spreads his mention. Al-Sa'di, on 'We raise in degrees whom We will' (12:76), adds that beneficial knowledge lifts its possessor above others and makes him a guide people follow. The clearest proof is the Prophet ﷺ himself: an orphan whose mention Allah raised to the call of every prayer (94:4).

closing_heading: Why this name stays with us
closing: We carry a quiet fear that we are stuck at the bottom, unseen and unlifted, while everyone else is carried up. Ar-Rafi is the answer the Qur'an gives, not as a comforting idea but as something Allah does on every page. The One raised above all degrees is the very One who reaches down to lift, and the people He lifts are not the ones standing tallest. As the mufassirun read Surah Al-Waqia, on the Day that makes everything true, He lowers the arrogant and raises the humble. He raised the believers and the people of knowledge by degrees, He raised an orphan's mention until it is spoken beside His own, and He still keeps lifting the ones the world walks past. The ladder is faith, knowledge, and a heart low enough to be raised. To know this name is to stop clawing upward against other people and to start going low, on purpose, for the only One whose lifting lasts.

O Allah, Ar-Rafi, the One who raises, You lower whom You will and lift whom You will, and the final word on every rank is Yours. Lift us by faith and by knowledge, raise us in the only degrees that last, keep us humble at every height because You are always higher, and make us among the lowly that You raise and not the proud that You bring low. Rabbi hab li hukman wa alhiqni bis-salihin, waj'alni min warathati jannatin-na'im.

slides:
Ar-Rafi.

The One who raises.

Almost all of us carry a private ledger of where we rank. The room we're afraid to enter. The name called before ours. The sense that others got lifted and we got left on the floor.

This name tells you who actually holds the rope.

The Qur'an does not leave rank in human hands.

Surah Al-Waqia sums up the whole Day of Judgement in two words:

khafidatun rafi'ah, **lowering, raising** (56:3).

One Hand does both. No throne is safe without Him. No floor is final with Him.

And here is the part that overturns everything.

Who gets raised?

Ibn Kathir gathers the early readings of 56:3. Al-Suddi's is the sharpest:

**'It lowers the arrogant, and it raises the humble.'**

The currency that buys height with Allah is the opposite of the world's.

The world lifts whoever pushes to the front.

Allah, on the Day all is made true, lifts the one who was content to be at the back for His sake.

So what is the ladder actually made of?

'Allah will raise those who have believed among you and those who were given knowledge, **by degrees**.' (58:11)

Faith and knowledge. Named outright.

The believers are told to make room, to give up their seat.

**'Whoever humbles himself for the command of Allah, Allah raises his rank and spreads his mention.'**

You climb by stooping.

Until there is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah, called from every minaret till the end of time. ﷺ

Ar-Rafi does not lift those who lifted themselves. He lifts the ones who lowered themselves to Him.

Ya Rafi.
social.caption:
Almost all of us keep a private ledger of where we rank: the room we're afraid to walk into, the name called before ours, the quiet fear that we've been left on the floor while everyone else got lifted.

Ar-Rafi, the One who raises, walks into that fear and tells you who holds the rope. Grounded in classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir and al-Sa'di), this name does not leave status in human hands. Surah Al-Waqia sums up the whole Day in two words: khafidatun rafi'ah, lowering and raising (56:3), one Hand doing both.

And the tafsir overturns the order we live by. Reading 56:3, Ibn Kathir gathers the early scholars, and al-Suddi puts it sharpest: 'it lowers the arrogant, and it raises the humble.' The ladder Allah lifts by is named outright: 'Allah will raise those who have believed among you and those who were given knowledge, by degrees' (58:11). And the rope is humility. Ibn Kathir's rule for the whole name: whoever humbles himself for the command of Allah, Allah raises his rank and spreads his mention.

The proof is the Prophet ﷺ himself, an orphan whose mention Allah raised until it is called from every minaret beside His own (94:4). He raised a freed slave over the nobles of Makkah for his knowledge. He keeps lifting the ones the world walks past.

(Note: the definite name-form الرافع is not a standing name in the Qur'an; it comes through the tradition of the ninety-nine and is grounded here in the verb and construct as they occur, e.g. 40:15, 58:11, 94:4.)

So stop clawing upward against other people. Go low, on purpose, for the One whose lifting lasts.

Where have you been afraid you're stuck? He raises from below. 🤍

email.subject: The One who lowers the proud and raises the humble
email.alt_subject: When you're afraid you're stuck at the bottom
email.preview: Ar-Rafi, the One who raises. The Qur'an takes rank out of human hands, and the tafsir says who actually gets lifted.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Almost all of us carry a private ledger of where we rank. We feel it in the room we are afraid to walk into, the name that gets called before ours, the quiet sense that other people have been lifted and we have been left on the floor. We spend a great deal of our lives trying to climb, and an exhausting amount of energy trying not to be brought low.

One of the names of Allah walks straight into that anxiety: Ar-Rafi, the One who raises.

First, an honest note. The definite name-form you see in the list of ninety-nine, Ar-Rafi, does not appear in the Qur'an as a standing name of Allah the way As-Sami or Al-Alim do. What the Qur'an gives is the action and the description: the verb rafa'a, to raise, used of Allah over and over, and the construct rafi' al-darajat, the One who raises ranks (Ghafir 40:15). The name comes to us through the tradition of the ninety-nine, built faithfully on what the Book repeatedly says Allah does. We would rather show you the seam than paper over it.

The Qur'an does not leave rank in human hands. Surah Al-Waqia sums up the whole Day of Judgement in two words: khafidatun rafi'ah, lowering and raising (56:3). One Hand does both. And here is the part that overturns the order we live by. Commenting on this verse, Ibn Kathir gathers the early scholars on who ends up where: the Hour lowers the mighty of this world and raises the lowly. Muhammad ibn Ka'b said it brings down men who were elevated in the dunya and raises men who were brought low. And al-Suddi put it most sharply: it lowers the arrogant, and it raises the humble.

So what is the ladder actually made of? The Qur'an names it: 'Allah will raise those who have believed among you and those who were given knowledge, by degrees' (Al-Mujadila 58:11). Faith and knowledge. And read the verse just before the lifting, where the believers are told to make room and give up their seat. Ibn Kathir draws out the rule of the whole name from it: do not think yielding your seat is a loss, it is rank with Allah, for whoever humbles himself for the command of Allah, Allah raises his rank and spreads his mention. You climb by stooping. The seat you gave up was never the height. Giving it up was.

If you want to see what Ar-Rafi can do, look at the highest thing He ever raised. To His Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, an orphan in Makkah who was mocked and driven out, Allah said: 'And We raised high for you your mention' (Ash-Sharh 94:4). Ibn Kathir relates that this means Allah is not mentioned except that he is mentioned alongside Him, until there is no god but Allah and Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah, called from every minaret to the end of time. The lowest beginning the world could offer, turned into the highest mention a human has ever held.

So here is the invitation of this name. You are not stuck at the bottom of anything. Stop clawing your way up against other people, and stop dreading being below them, because the only height that lasts is the one Ar-Rafi grants, and He grants it to the humble. Go low, on purpose, for His sake. Make room. Take the lesser seat. Seek the faith and the knowledge He lifts by. The times you lowered yourself for Allah and no one noticed were not lost. He raises from below.

Ya Rafi, lift us by faith and by knowledge, raise us in the only degrees that last, and make us among the humble You raise.
